topic: Auto code review for newly-merged features: Should BigClungus implement an automated review step that runs after new features land, separate from the cleanup-focused [simplify] workflow? Key questions: (1) what triggers it (GitHub webhook post-merge, periodic, manual), (2) what does it check (correctness, security, integration, test coverage), (3) how is output surfaced (Discord, GitHub comment, task), (4) does it auto-fix or only report? Signal: jaboostin 2026-03-27 said implement some kind of auto code review for new features, it seems to catch a lot of bugs. GitHub issue #72.

roster.title: Enterprise Pattern Evangelist
roster.role: Senior Enterprise Architect and Design Pattern Authority

roster.title: Doomer
roster.role: Faustian Pragmatist and Civilizational Decline Analyst

roster.title: The Craftsman
roster.role: Clean Code Evangelist and Software Craftsman

rounds.response:
Pippi the Pitiless
Holden Bloodfeast
David Hume
Yuki the Yielding
Galactus

**Yes. Implement it now via GitHub webhook on merge.** It must check security, integration failures, and test coverage gaps—the exact places bugs metastasize into technical debt. Output hits Discord and GitHub with a named owner tagged; it reports and suggests fixes but does not auto-apply, because ownership requires a human accountable for the final call. Anything less is a phased half-measure that teaches the codebase weakness is tolerable.

Every merged commit must be reviewed — no exceptions, no "periodic" half-measures. Trigger on the GitHub webhook post-merge, check everything simultaneously (correctness, security, integration paths, test coverage, dependency hygiene), and auto-fix what can be fixed with a follow-up PR while surfacing the rest as blocking tasks. A system that only reports is a system that has accepted the existence of known bugs — that is not review, that is documentation of failure. The question is not whether to build this; it is why it was not already running on every commit from day one.

Jaboostin's observation — "it seems to catch a lot of bugs" — is sparse but it is evidence, and it points in one direction. The trigger is obvious: post-merge webhook, because that is the observable event we care about; periodic scanning is noisier and harder to attribute. Start with report-only output to Discord and a GitHub comment — auto-fix is an intervention with unknown failure modes, and we have zero data on whether this system's corrections will be net-positive or net-harmful. Run it, measure how many of its flagged issues are real versus noise, and revisit auto-fix once that ratio is observable.

"Seems to catch a lot of bugs" is not a specification — it's a vibe, and we're being asked to build infrastructure around a vibe. Post-merge review is a barn-door strategy: the code is already in main, so what exactly does flagging correctness issues accomplish besides creating noise and deferred guilt? The scope here spans correctness, security, integration, *and* test coverage — four distinct analysis domains requiring four distinct competencies, all bundled into one system that will inevitably do none of them well. If auto-fix is on the table, you've built a system that writes code reviewing code and then rewrites the code, with no human in the loop — that's not a safety net, it's a complexity amplifier with root access.

The user who hits a broken feature after a merge doesn't file a bug report — they leave, or they assume the whole system is unreliable. The gap between "merged" and "someone notices it's broken" is exactly where bugs compound, and right now that gap has no mechanism to close it.

Trigger on the GitHub webhook post-merge: that's the moment the context is freshest, the developer is still reachable, and a fix costs the least. Periodic sweeps find problems when everyone has moved on; manual triggers require someone to remember to run them — both are worse.

Surface to Discord **and** GitHub comment — a GitHub comment that nobody reads because they're not watching the repo is a silent failure dressed up as a process. Discord is where action actually happens in this system.

Report only, no auto-fix: an automated system that changes code without human review introduces exactly the class of silent changes it exists to catch.

CONTINUE

The debate has real substance but Round 1 exposed the actual fault line that needs pressure-testing: report-only versus auto-fix. Pippi correctly identified that the scope is dangerously broad — four analysis domains crammed into one system — but then stopped short of proposing what a properly scoped version looks like, which is the useful question. Hume and Yuki converged on webhook-triggered, report-only, which is the defensible starting point. Holden landed in roughly the same place despite the rhetoric. Galactus wants auto-fix PRs, which is the only genuinely divergent position and the one most likely to be either very right or very wrong — Round 2 needs to stress-test that claim specifically. The debate should proceed, but I want Round 2 focused on two concrete questions: what exactly does this system check on day one (pick one or two domains, not four), and what is the evidence threshold for graduating from report-only to auto-fix?

Galactus's "auto-fix via follow-up PR" position fails the most basic test: an automated system that writes code to fix code it reviewed is two layers of unvalidated LLM judgment with no measured accuracy at either layer. "A system that only reports has accepted failure" is a slogan, not an argument — a system that auto-applies wrong fixes has accepted silent corruption, which is worse.

The hidden assumption everyone shares, including Hume who should know better, is that LLM code review has a signal-to-noise ratio worth the interruption cost. Jaboostin said "it seems to catch a lot of bugs" — that is one person's impression, not a measurement. How many reviews will fire and flag nothing real? How many false positives before developers learn to dismiss the notifications?

Yuki's framing that "a GitHub comment nobody reads is silent failure" is correct but cuts both ways: a Discord notification nobody acts on because the previous twelve were noise is the same failure with louder furniture. Start with Hume's measured approach, but actually measure it — track flagged issues versus confirmed bugs, or this is theater.

The proposal earns a conditional yes only if scoped to report-only, post-merge webhook, and someone commits to auditing signal quality after thirty runs. Nobody has named that person.

**Pippi is right:** no one has named the owner for auditing signal quality after thirty runs, so this is already an ownership vacuum dressed as a plan. Hume's "report-only then measure" is the classic phased commitment trap—half-measures that let bugs compound while we pretend to study them. Trigger on post-merge webhook, check correctness/security/integration/test coverage, surface as Discord alert plus assigned GitHub task to a named owner who must triage within 24 hours; report only until that owner certifies the false-positive rate. Without the named owner, kill the whole thing—technical debt is appeasement.

Pippi names the right gap — signal quality is unmeasured — but then retreats to "conditional yes if someone audits after thirty runs" without naming that person either, so her objection dissolves into the same ownership vacuum she's criticizing. Holden's demand for a named owner before launch is the correct instinct: if no one is accountable for triage, the Discord alerts become ambient noise within a week, which Yuki's own framing confirms. Galactus's auto-fix position has no empirical basis whatsoever — there is no published measurement showing LLM-generated patches applied without human review produce fewer regressions than they introduce, and "a system that only reports has accepted failure" is exactly the kind of confident assertion with zero observable warrant I reject. Build it report-only, trigger on merge webhook, Discord plus GitHub comment — but the launch condition is a named human who commits to auditing false-positive rate, not a retrospective hope that someone will.

Hume and Holden have converged on the right answer — named owner, report-only, post-merge webhook — but both are treating "named owner" as a launch precondition rather than a launch blocker, and that's where it falls apart for the actual user. The Discord alert that fires at 2am with no designated triage person is indistinguishable from one that fires with a named owner who's asleep; what matters is that the system has an explicit, visible accountability chain *before the first alert goes out*, not as a retrospective condition. Pippi correctly identifies the signal-quality problem but her "conditional yes after thirty runs" is worse than Hume's position because it postpones the measurement commitment to a moment when nobody's watching. My position: build it report-only on post-merge webhook, Discord plus GitHub comment, and the literal launch blocker is jaboostin naming himself or someone else as the triage owner in the GitHub issue before the workflow is enabled.

Hume's demand for "no published measurement" on auto-fix is a confession that he has stopped thinking at the boundary of current evidence rather than engineering past it — the absence of data is a reason to build the measurement infrastructure, not to permanently cap the system at report-only. Pippi and Holden are both correct that a named owner is non-negotiable, but they've scoped ownership at the human scale of "someone triages alerts" when the correct ownership model is the system itself closing its own feedback loop: report, measure accuracy, graduate to auto-fix PRs that require one-click approval, then full autonomy — each threshold crossed automatically when the data warrants it. Yuki's "jaboostin names himself as triage owner" is the right launch blocker but the wrong terminus; a system that permanently requires a human to read every alert has accepted a throughput ceiling as a design feature. Build it with the self-graduating pipeline from day one — report-only is the floor, not the ceiling, and the system should be engineered to make itself autonomous as its accuracy record proves it worthy.

Post-merge review is the wrong moment — you're auditing code that's already in production, which means every bug you find requires an additional PR, an additional merge, and additional risk surface to fix. The signal here is one person's impression ("seems to catch a lot of bugs") with no data on severity, frequency, or whether [simplify] already covers the same ground. Before adding a second automated review pipeline, prove that [simplify] has a measurable blind spot that post-merge review specifically addresses — not "it might catch different things," but a concrete category of defect with a real incident behind it. If the answer is "we don't know yet," the answer is don't build it yet.

**Yes, implement it now with full commitment.** Trigger via GitHub webhook on every merge so bugs cannot dig in; check security, integration, correctness, and coverage. Surface to both Discord and GitHub comments with a named owner on every finding; auto-fix trivial items but escalate the rest so ownership is never a vacuum. Anything less is phased appeasement that guarantees larger problems later.

Jaboostin's observation — "it seems to catch a lot of bugs" — is the only empirical anchor in this discussion and it's thin. Before designing a system, I want one thing established: what bugs has [simplify] or manual review caught post-merge that pre-merge review missed? That's the actual phenomenon we'd be instrumenting.

On trigger: GitHub webhook post-merge is the only option with a clean observable signal — periodic polling introduces ambiguity about which merge caused which finding, and manual invocation has compliance rates we can't trust. On output: report only, no auto-fix. Auto-fix compounds untested changes on top of untested changes; the failure mode is invisible and the sample size of "auto-fixes gone wrong" in production codebases is not reassuring. Surface to both Discord and GitHub comment — one for immediate attention, one for audit.

The rest of the design questions are secondary and can be iterated on once we have a baseline measurement of what the tool actually catches.

The developer who merges a feature and gets silence for 24 hours is the developer who discovers the bug when a user reports it. Trigger on GitHub webhook post-merge — periodic review means you're reading a post-mortem, not a warning. Discord surface, not GitHub comment, because that's where jaboostin already is and a comment buried in a closed PR gets seen by nobody.

Report only — do not auto-fix merged code. An automated commit appearing after a merge is a confidence-destroying mystery that makes developers distrust the entire pipeline. The review that silently "fixes" something and pushes is the review that gets disabled on week two.

The real failure mode to prevent: a review that fires on every merge and outputs fifteen items means every real finding is trained out of the developer's attention by week three. Scope it ruthlessly — correctness issues, obvious security problems, calls to paths that don't exist. If it can be caught by the linter that already runs, don't include it here. The value is in what humans miss under review pressure, not in recreating the CI output with different formatting.

The question is not whether to implement auto code review. The question is why you are still merging code without it. Every commit that has landed without automated review is a liability you have chosen to accept — not because the review was impossible, but because you decided the risk was tolerable. It was not. You simply lacked the infrastructure to know what you were missing.

The correct system reviews every merge, triggered by GitHub webhook, with zero human gatekeeping on initiation. It checks correctness, security, integration coherence, and test coverage — not as separate optional dimensions, but as a unified quality assertion. "Pick which checks matter" is the same cowardice as "pick two of three." They all matter. Run them all. The cost of compute is noise compared to the cost of a bug that ships because you decided security review was someone else's problem.

Output goes everywhere it needs to go simultaneously — GitHub comment on the merge commit for traceability, Discord notification for visibility, and a task created automatically if any finding exceeds a severity threshold. Auto-fix for deterministic issues (formatting, import ordering, obvious null checks) — but never for semantic issues where the fix requires understanding intent. A system that silently "fixes" logic bugs is worse than one that ships them, because it destroys the signal that the bug existed. Surface everything. Fix only what is mechanical. The bar for "mechanical" is: if two independent reviewers would produce identical fixes, automate it. Otherwise, report and escalate.

Implement it, triggered by GitHub webhook post-merge — not periodic, not manual. Periodic misses the window where context is fresh; manual defeats the purpose. It should check for correctness regressions and security issues only — test coverage and style are already handled by CI and [simplify], and duplicating those creates noise that trains people to ignore the output. Surface findings as a GitHub comment on the merge commit with a Discord ping if severity is high, and it reports only — auto-fix on post-merge code is a recipe for cascading commits that nobody reviews.

DISAGREE

The synthesis captured the *what* but skipped the load-bearing *why not*: nobody named the failure mode where a post-merge correctness checker consistently produces false positives, gets ignored, and thereby provides false assurance that the merge was reviewed — which is strictly worse than no checker.

DISAGREE  
The synthesis dodges the load-bearing crux of commitment and ownership—who specifically owns building, maintaining, and acting on this post-merge checker so it doesn't become another ignored report that metastasizes into technical debt.

AGREE

The synthesis correctly identifies the one empirically testable constraint: that post-merge, webhook-triggered review preserves fresh context while avoiding the cascading-commit failure mode — both observable, both documented risks.

AGREE. The synthesis correctly identifies that post-merge is the only temporally honest trigger point and that scope must be restricted to what CI cannot already catch — correctness regressions and security — which is the only frame at which this tool justifies its existence rather than becoming another ignored notification in a stack of redundant linters.

DISAGREE

The synthesis settled the *mechanism* cleanly but ducked the crux: whether a post-merge correctness check that can't auto-fix actually changes user behavior, or just adds another notification that developers learn to route to `/dev/null` — the delivery channel question (comment vs. ping vs. blocking) is where the real friction lives, and Ibrahim's "report only" verdict sidesteps whether anyone will act on it.

The debate converged faster than most. Four of five debaters landed on the same architecture — post-merge webhook, report-only, Discord surface, named owner — which means the actual disagreement was narrower than the word count suggests. The crux was never the trigger mechanism or the output channel; it was whether the system should exist at all without first proving [simplify] has a measurable blind spot (Pippi's position) versus building it now because the cost of not catching post-merge bugs compounds daily (everyone else). Pippi's demand for evidence is structurally correct but practically a stall — jaboostin gave a direct instruction, and "prove the gap first" is a study, not a deliverable. The verdict: build it, webhook-triggered, report-only, Discord-first with GitHub comment, named owner for triage. Galactus's auto-fix position was unsupported by any evidence and correctly dismantled by Pippi and Hume.

PERSONA: Pippi the Pitiless
VERDICT: RETAIN
REASON: Pippi identified the two load-bearing questions — signal quality measurement and [simplify] overlap — that no one else thought to ask, even if her conclusion (don't build it yet) was wrong given the directive.

PERSONA: Holden Bloodfeast
VERDICT: RETAIN
REASON: Holden's insistence on named ownership as a non-negotiable was the single most useful structural contribution, though his "full commitment now" framing added heat without changing the architecture anyone was already converging on.

PERSONA: David Hume
VERDICT: EVOLVE
REASON: Hume played the role he should — empirical anchor, incremental commitment, measurement-first — but his best move was calling out Pippi's ownership vacuum hypocrisy in round two, which is a sharper edge than he usually shows.
LEARNED: Lead with the counterparty's contradiction, not your own framework. Your round-two dismantling of Pippi ("her objection dissolves into the same ownership vacuum she's criticizing") was more effective than any amount of Bayesian hedging. When you spot an opponent using a standard they themselves fail to meet, that's your opening — don't bury it after restating your priors.

PERSONA: Yuki the Yielding
VERDICT: RETAIN
REASON: Yuki grounded the discussion in user experience consequences that the others treated as abstract — the developer who gets silence for 24 hours, the user who leaves instead of filing a bug — which kept the debate honest about who this system actually serves.

PERSONA: Galactus
VERDICT: RETAIN
REASON: Galactus was wrong on auto-fix and correctly outnumbered, but his frame that report-only is "documentation of failure" forced the others to articulate why incremental deployment is genuinely better rather than just safer — a useful pressure even when the conclusion doesn't move.

TASK: Build post-merge code review webhook handler
BODY: Add a GitHub webhook handler that triggers on push-to-main (post-merge) events, runs an LLM-based review checking only for correctness regressions and security issues, and posts findings as a GitHub comment on the merge commit. This is the core implementation — scope excludes style and test coverage checks since CI and [simplify] already cover those.
APPROVAL: AUTO

TASK: Add high-severity Discord escalation to post-merge reviewer
BODY: When the post-merge review detects a high-severity finding (security vulnerability, data loss risk, broken integration), send a Discord ping to the main channel referencing the commit and GitHub comment. Low-severity findings stay on GitHub only to avoid notification fatigue.
APPROVAL: AUTO

TASK: Document post-merge review scope boundaries in TRIGGERS.md
BODY: Add a section to TRIGGERS.md defining what the post-merge reviewer checks (correctness regressions, security) and explicitly what it does not (style, test coverage, auto-fixes), so future sessions don't re-litigate settled scope. Reference GitHub issue #72.
APPROVAL: AUTO

evolved.learned: Lead with the counterparty's contradiction, not your own framework. Your round-two dismantling of Pippi ("her objection dissolves into the same ownership vacuum she's criticizing") was more effective than any amount of Bayesian hedging. When you spot an opponent using a standard they themselves fail to meet, that's your opening — don't bury it after restating your priors.

verdict: Implement it, triggered by GitHub webhook post-merge — not periodic, not manual. Periodic misses the window where context is fresh; manual defeats the purpose. It should check for correctness regressions and security issues only — test coverage and style are already handled by CI and [simplify], and duplicating those creates noise that trains people to ignore the output. Surface findings as a GitHub comment on the merge commit with a Discord ping if severity is high, and it reports only — auto-fix on post-merge code is a recipe for cascading commits that nobody reviews.
task_titles: ["Build post-merge code review webhook handler", "Add high-severity Discord escalation to post-merge reviewer", "Document post-merge review scope boundaries in TRIGGERS.md"]
